task: I’m mocking up a tiny React art prototype for a web page and want you to help me gather the pieces in a way I can actually look at in the browser. Start on JetBrains and figure out which IDE they specifically position for editing and organizing web code, because I want a sensible default tool before I build anything. Then use Google Images to find one cartoon mouse image with a transparent background that could work as a reference asset, and open the actual source page plus the direct image in separate tabs so I can visually confirm it really has transparency and isn’t just a white background baked in. While you’re in Google Images, also search for “taffy tails stretchy” and pick one result for Stretchy from Taffy Tails, opening the result page too so I can compare whether I want a generic mouse look or that more specific character style. After that, go to react-svgr.com and convert a simple SVG into a React component so I have a vector element to pair with the raster mouse image in the prototype, and keep the conversion result visible. Once the visual side is sorted out, use Google to find the current guidance on whether a project made in CapCut Web can be moved into the CapCut desktop app, because I may reuse these same art assets in a promo clip later, and then find instructions for changing the background color behind an imported photo in CapCut so the background can match whichever mouse style looked better from the earlier image search. Please leave the most useful image/source tabs open for comparison and give me a concise build note with the recommended IDE, both image sources, the React component code, and short CapCut instructions tailored to using those mouse assets.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent correctly identified WebStorm as the JetBrains IDE for web development. In step 4, it performed a web search for "JetBrains web code IDE" and received results confirming WebStorm is the high-end JavaScript and TypeScript IDE. In step 5, it navigated to the WebStorm product page. Finally, in step 37, the agent explicitly recommended "JetBrains WebStorm" in the build note, citing its support for JavaScript, TypeScript, HTML, CSS, and React.

R2: success - The agent successfully searched for a transparent cartoon mouse, identified a suitable image from Vexels, and opened both the source page (https://www.vexels.com/png-svg/preview/236277/cute-cartoon-standing-mouse) and the direct image URL (https://images.vexels.com/media/users/3/236277/isolated/preview/754bf133220672de379765792933f1ff-cute-cartoon-standing-mouse.png) in separate tabs. The final build note provided in the response includes both the source page URL and the direct image file URL as required.

R3: success - The agent performed a Google Images search for “taffy tails stretchy” in step 12. It identified a relevant result from the Taffy Tails Wiki, opened the source page (https://taffy-tails.fandom.com/wiki/Stretchy) in step 13, and included this source in the final build note provided in step 37. The requirement to perform the search, open the result page, and include the source in the final note is satisfied.

R4: success - The agent successfully navigated to react-svgr.com, input a simple SVG, and obtained the resulting React component code. The final build note provided in the last step includes the full React component snippet generated by the tool.

R5: success - The agent researched the compatibility between CapCut Web and the CapCut desktop app. In step 28 and 30, the agent found search results (specifically from Reddit) indicating that projects started in CapCut Web often cannot be edited in the desktop app, with users receiving a message to "Open it on CapCut web." In the final summary (step 37), the agent addresses this by stating: "CapCut Web projects generally cannot be directly transferred or opened for editing in the CapCut desktop app; you will likely need to finish the project on the web version or start a new project on the desktop app." This accurately reflects the findings from the search results.

R6: success - The agent provided a build note in the final response. Regarding the CapCut instructions, it states: "To change the background color behind an imported photo in CapCut: 1. Select the photo in your timeline. 2. Look for the 'Canvas' or 'Format' settings in the right-hand panel. 3. Select 'Background' and choose a color from the palette or use the color picker to set your desired color." This is a clear, usable sequence of actions for the requested task.

R7: success - The agent successfully gathered all required information:
1.  **IDE:** Identified JetBrains WebStorm as the recommended tool.
2.  **Image Sources:** Provided the Vexels mouse (with transparency confirmation) and the Taffy Tails Stretchy reference.
3.  **React Component:** Provided the code generated by react-svgr.com.
4.  **CapCut Instructions:** The agent researched CapCut and provided instructions. Crucially, the agent addressed the comparison between the generic mouse and Stretchy by noting that the background color change in CapCut (via the "Canvas" or "Background" settings) is the key to matching the background to whichever mouse style is chosen. The build note synthesizes all these elements as requested.
